do: use `solid / primary` (ctrl-brand) for the single most important action — the one you want the user to take
pair `primary` with `subtle` or `ghost` for secondary actions — not with another `primary`
use `outline` when you need multiple side-by-side buttons with elevated prominence
dont: use multiple `primary` buttons side-by-side — it overwhelms users and dilutes the hierarchy
use `secondary` (ctrl-neutral) for `<a>` links that jump users out of the current journey
use `subtle` on accent/colored backgrounds — it's designed for non-accent backgrounds only
a11y: Buttons must be focusable and activated with `Enter` or `Space`
Focus state (`state: focus`) uses the `focus outline` dependency for visible focus ring
interaction: Click/tap: Triggers the assigned action on pointer up
Hover: Background shifts to hover token on pointer enter; reverts on pointer leave
assembly: Maintain consistent sizing within the same context (don't mix `sm` and `lg` side by side)
Primary action button should be visually prominent (use `solid` variant)
whenToUse: To initiate a single action (submit, navigate, confirm, cancel)
As a call-to-action within cards, sections, or overlays
For navigation triggers (e.g., "See all", "View more")
whenNotToUse: For navigation to external URLs with no action — use a link instead
For toggling a state on/off — use Toggle
For selecting from a set of options — use Radio button or Check box
For filtering content — use Filter item

do: use tooltips to label icon-only controls (pair with `aria-label` on the control)
default to `Top` direction and adjust only when space requires it
keep content short and scannable
dont: put interactive elements (links, buttons) inside tooltips
use tooltips for essential information — it's hidden until hover and inaccessible on touch devices
show tooltips on elements that already have visible, descriptive labels
a11y: Tooltip must use `role="tooltip"` and be associated via `aria-describedby` on the trigger
Tooltip must appear on both hover and keyboard focus (`focus` state on trigger)
interaction: Hover trigger: Tooltip bubble appears on pointer enter over the icon; hides on pointer leave
Focus trigger: Tooltip appears on keyboard focus; hides on blur
assembly: Choose direction based on available space — avoid tooltips that extend beyond the viewport
Default to `Top` direction; fall back to `bottom`, `Left`, or `Right` when space is constrained
whenToUse: To provide additional context for icon-only buttons or controls
To explain truncated text on hover
To display supplementary information that doesn't warrant permanent screen space
whenNotToUse: For critical information the user must see — display it inline instead
For interactive content (links, buttons) — use a Popover or Flyout
For form validation messages — use inline error states
For long-form content — use a Flyout or expandable section

do: always include a visible close button in the Header
trap focus within the Drawer when it is open
return focus to the triggering element when the Drawer closes
dont: open a Drawer from within another Drawer or Overlay
use Drawer for critical workflow steps that require full attention — use Overlay
assume side-by-side viewing is always available — full-screen on mobile
a11y: Focus is trapped within the Drawer (Tab cycles through internal elements only)
`Escape` key dismisses the Drawer
interaction: Trigger action opens the Drawer with a slide-in animation from the right edge
Click the Backdrop or press Escape to dismiss the Drawer
assembly: Drawer slides in from the right edge of the viewport
The 424px width is fixed across all desktop/tablet breakpoints — it does not scale
whenToUse: For detail views that should coexist with the main page (e.g., clicking a card to expand)
For settings panels and configuration UI
For progressive disclosure of additional content
When users need to reference both the main page and supplementary content
whenNotToUse: For focused, interruptive tasks — use Overlay instead
For lightweight tooltips or popups — use Flyout
For inline content expansion — use Section Footer "Expansion" action

do: always include a visible close button in the Header
trap focus within the Overlay when it is open
return focus to the triggering element when the Overlay closes
dont: open an Overlay from within another Overlay (no stacking)
use Overlay for content that should persist alongside the page — use Drawer
auto-open Overlays without user action
a11y: Focus is trapped within the Overlay (Tab cycles through internal elements only)
`Escape` key dismisses the Overlay
interaction: Trigger action opens the Overlay with a fade-in/scale-up animation centered on screen
Click the Backdrop or press Escape to dismiss the Overlay
assembly: Overlay is centered horizontally and vertically on desktop/tablet
Backdrop covers the full viewport behind the modal
whenToUse: For focused, interruptive tasks that require user attention (confirmations, detail views)
When content needs to be presented over the current page without navigating away
For expanded card views, image galleries, or video players
whenNotToUse: For side-panel content — use Drawer instead
For inline content expansion — use Section Footer "Expansion" action
For lightweight tooltips or popups — use Flyout instead

click action.values: No action, Expansion: See less, Expansion: See more, Redirect

do: use Section Footer to close every section — even if only using `No action` with Feedback
pair "Expansion: See more" and "Expansion: See less" as toggle states
use `No action` + `Feedback=true` for standalone feedback within a section
dont: place the Feedback module anywhere other than inside Section Footer
use multiple click actions simultaneously — only one CTA per footer
use "Expansion: See less" without a corresponding "See more" state
a11y: CTA button (Redirect/Expansion) must be focusable and activated with `Enter` or `Space`
Feedback thumbs must be individually focusable
interaction: Expansion: See more \u2014 Expands the section body beyond the default 424px height; body height becomes dynamic (hug content), pushing content below downward; button label and chevron toggle to \u2018See less\u2019 (\u2191)
Expansion: See less \u2014 Collapses section body back to 424px; button label and chevron toggle back to \u2018See more\u2019 (\u2193); the two Expansion states always appear as a paired toggle
assembly: Section Footer always appears as the last child in a Section, after the body type
When `block?=true`, the footer renders as a full-width block element
whenToUse: As the last child of any Section component (after Section Title + body type)
When a section needs a CTA ("See more", redirect link)
When user feedback (thumbs up/down) should be collected for a section
When source attribution or footnotes are required
whenNotToUse: As a standalone footer outside a Section — use Page footer instead
For card-level actions — use card footer patterns
For standalone feedback — feedback only appears within Section Footer

do: use `soft` variant as the default — it integrates smoothly with surrounding content without drawing excessive attention
use consistent semantic colors across the product so users learn to associate color with meaning (e.g., green = success everywhere)
pair icons or favicons with labels for accessibility — the label provides context that icons alone cannot
dont: overuse badges — more than 2–3 per card creates visual noise and diminishes their impact
use `danger` color for non-critical information — reserve it for errors, expired states, or genuinely urgent alerts
use `solid` variant for every badge — overuse of high-emphasis badges defeats the purpose of visual hierarchy
a11y: Badge text should be announced as part of the surrounding content flow
When `showLabel` is `false`, ensure the badge's meaning is conveyed through adjacent content or a visually hidden `aria-label`
interaction: Non-interactive display element \u2014 no pointer events by default
When placed inside an interactive container, inherits parent interaction
assembly: Maintain consistent badge sizing within the same context (don't mix `sm` and `md` side by side)
Limit badges per card — 1–2 badges per item keeps the interface clean; 3+ creates visual clutter
whenToUse: To indicate status or severity (e.g., "Critical", "New", "Updated")
To categorize or tag content within cards and list results
To highlight a key attribute (source type, content freshness, risk level)
On top of images or media to label overlaid content
whenNotToUse: For interactive actions — use Button instead
For toggling a state on/off — use Toggle
For navigation or links — use Button or link
For long-form descriptions — badges should be concise (1–3 words)

do: always use the **line style** (outline) as the default — never fill/solid style unless specifically required
source all icons from the **FluentUI icon library** — do not use icons from other libraries
ensure the icon glyph is **registered in Bing SNR** before using it in production designs
dont: use fill/solid style icons — line style is the ACF standard
use icons from non-FluentUI sources — all glyphs must come from the FluentUI icon set
use an icon that has not been registered in Bing SNR — unregistered icons will not render correctly in production
a11y: Icons inside interactive components inherit accessibility from the parent
Standalone decorative icons should be marked as aria-hidden="true"
interaction: Icons are non-interactive — interaction is handled by the parent component
Hover, pressed, and focus states are inherited from the wrapping element
assembly: Always match iconSize to the parent component's size context
The icon page in Figma contains the full glyph library under the iconStyle frame
whenToUse: Inside interactive components (Button, Tab, Accordion chevron) as a visual affordance
As standalone decorative or informational indicators
To reinforce meaning alongside text labels
whenNotToUse: As the sole means of conveying critical information — always pair with a text label or `ariaLabel`
For complex illustrations or imagery — use Image or Thumbnail instead
As clickable elements on their own — wrap in a Button (icon-only variant) or Ads menu icon

do: use fuzzy Mark for content flagged as sensitive by the content pipeline
ensure images have appropriate alt text in the consuming component
maintain aspect ratios consistent with the parent card type
dont: use Image standalone for decorative purposes — use frame fills instead
rely solely on fuzzy mark for content moderation — it's a visual indicator, not a blocker
a11y: Images must have descriptive alt text provided by the consuming component
Fuzzy-marked images should indicate their obscured state to assistive technology
interaction: Image is non-interactive — interaction is handled by the parent card or container
Fuzzy mark may be toggled programmatically based on content classification
assembly: Image is typically consumed inside higher-level card components — rarely placed standalone
**Fully occupied:** When used as a card hero or standalone visual, set the image to fill the entire container frame — no padding or gap
whenToUse: As the base image element inside card components (image card, video card, multi-image carousel)
When displaying user-generated or editorial imagery that may need fuzzy marking
Inside Thumbnail as the underlying image renderer
whenNotToUse: For icons or glyphs — use Icon instead
For avatar/profile pictures — use Avatar
For decorative backgrounds — apply fills directly to frames

do: truncate at a maximum line count that preserves meaning (typically 2–3 lines)
make truncation feel deliberate — the ellipsis should suggest "more content available"
ensure expanded text is re-collapsible (if expand/collapse pattern is used)
dont: truncate titles — titles should generally be fully visible (or use a different strategy)
truncate text mid-word — cut at word boundaries
hide critical information behind truncation with no expand option
a11y: Navigation link must be focusable via Tab
Activated via Enter — opens source page in a new tab
interaction: Click/tap: Opens the source detail page in a new browser tab — this is a navigation link, not an inline expand
Hover: Text link shows hover state styling
assembly: Place Truncation at the end of the card body text, after the ellipsis ('…') that clips the content
The trigger ('Continue reading →') is a navigation link that opens the full source page in a new tab — it does NOT expand text inline
whenToUse: When text content may exceed available space (card descriptions, answer text)
When users should be able to expand truncated content inline
For consistent truncation behavior across cards and sections
whenNotToUse: When text should always be fully visible — don't truncate
For section-level expand/collapse — use Section Footer "Expansion" action
For content that fits within its container — no truncation needed

do: show skeletons immediately when content begins loading
match skeleton shapes to the approximate layout of real content
transition smoothly from skeleton to loaded content (no jarring replacement)
dont: show skeletons for content that loads nearly instantly
use generic rectangles that don't hint at content structure
combine skeleton and loaded content in the same section simultaneously (all or nothing)
a11y: Skeleton placeholders should not be focusable — they are non-interactive
When content loads, focus should not jump unexpectedly
interaction: Non-interactive: Skeleton is a loading placeholder; no user action available
Animation: Shimmer animation plays continuously until replaced by real content
assembly: Skeletons should occupy the same space as the content they replace to prevent layout shift
Maintain consistent spacing between skeleton elements matching the loaded layout
whenToUse: As a placeholder while content is loading (cards, sections, text blocks)
To maintain layout stability during asynchronous content loading
When the approximate shape of incoming content is known
whenNotToUse: For error states — use error messaging patterns instead
For empty states — use empty state patterns
When load time is imperceptible (<200ms) — showing a skeleton adds visual noise

do: use descriptive header text so users can scan and decide what to expand
default to collapsed (`expanded?=false`) to save vertical space
allow multiple accordions to be expanded simultaneously unless space is critical
dont: nest accordions inside accordions — it creates confusing navigation depth
hide critical information that users must see — put it outside the accordion
use accordion for very short content that doesn't benefit from collapsing
a11y: Accordion header should use `role="button"` with `aria-expanded="true/false"`
Body content should be in a region with `aria-labelledby` pointing to the header
interaction: Expand/collapse: Click or tap the header row to toggle the body content visibility
Chevron rotation: The chevron icon animates between down (collapsed) and up (expanded) states
assembly: Accordion items stack vertically with consistent spacing
The body slot uses the Basic component — configure its direction (row/col) as needed
whenToUse: Currently used primarily for **PAA (People Also Ask)** scenarios on Bing SERP
To organize related content into collapsible sections (e.g., FAQ, specifications)
To reduce vertical space by hiding secondary content behind expandable headers
To let users selectively reveal details without navigating away
For progressive disclosure in dense information layouts (answer cards, knowledge panels)
whenNotToUse: For primary navigation — use Tab instead
For single expand/collapse — use Expansion card instead
For toggling a boolean state — use Toggle
When all content should be visible at once — use a flat list or sections

do: keep tab labels concise and parallel in structure ("Images" / "Videos" / "News")
ensure the body content panel matches the selected tab's context
use left icons consistently — either all tabs have them, or none do
dont: nest Tablists within Tablists — use a different navigation pattern for sub-levels
use more than 6-8 tabs without ensuring the Horizontal Scroller handles overflow gracefully
mix Tab styles (Ghost and Solid) within the same Tablist
a11y: The outer container must have `role="tablist"`
Each Tab item must have `role="tab"` with `aria-selected` reflecting its state
interaction: Click or tap a Tab to switch the visible body panel
Arrow keys move between Tab items within the Horizontal Scroller
assembly: Tablist wires together Tab + Horizontal Scroller + Basic — use it instead of assembling manually
The body panel is a slot — drag your content design into it and remove placeholder text
whenToUse: To provide tabbed navigation with associated content panels
When content categories are mutually exclusive and parallel in hierarchy
For top-level navigation within a section or module (e.g., Wholepage tabs)
whenNotToUse: For primary site navigation — use a navigation bar or header links
For filtering content within a single view — use Filter item
For triggering actions — use Button
For toggling a binary state — use Toggle
When only a single Tab item is needed with no panel switching — use Tab directly

do: set `Overflow=Yes` when items exceed container width — this enables scroll arrows
use `gradient fade` to visually indicate more content is available off-screen
ensure the first visible item is fully rendered at rest (not partially clipped)
dont: use a scroller with only 1–2 items — there's nothing to scroll
hide critical content at the end of a long scroll — users may not reach it
disable gradient fade without good reason — it's a key affordance
a11y: Arrow buttons must be focusable and operable via `Enter`/`Space`
Left/Right arrow keys should scroll content when the scroller has focus
interaction: Scroll buttons: Click left/right arrows to scroll the content by one page width
Touch/drag: On touch devices, swipe horizontally to scroll content
assembly: Scroller occupies the full width of its parent container
Internal items use consistent horizontal gap
whenToUse: For horizontally-arranged repeating items that exceed container width (card carousels, thumbnail strips)
Inside card body areas or section body areas
When content should be browsable via left/right scroll rather than pagination
whenNotToUse: For vertical lists — use standard stacking layout
When all items fit within the container width (no overflow) — render flat
For single-item content — no scrolling needed

style.values: Clickable title, Text title, Copilot Branding

Props (title End Props).values: Sponsor, AI buttons, See more, Tabs, WP Tabs

do: use Section Title for every section — it provides scannability and accessible landmarks
keep heading text short and descriptive
use the action slot sparingly — only when a clear navigation target exists
dont: use Section Title without a parent Section container
place multiple Section Titles within a single Section
use the subtitle slot for long descriptions — keep it to a few words
a11y: If the action slot contains an interactive link, it must be focusable via Tab
Focus ring uses the standard `focus outline` dependency
interaction: Structural container \u2014 interaction is delegated to child components
Layout responds to viewport changes via responsive breakpoints
assembly: Always position as the first element inside a Section, above the body type
Section Title spans the full width of the section container
whenToUse: As the first child of any Section component
To label a content group (e.g., "Top stories", "People also ask", "Related")
When a section needs a subtitle or inline action (e.g., "See all")
whenNotToUse: As a standalone heading outside of a Section — use standard typography instead
For card-level titles — use card title patterns
For page-level headings — use the Page header
> **endProps slot:** Optionally shows tab-like pills (Wholepage tabs) that requery the page according to topics related to the answer.

do: use `subtle` for within-card separation to maintain a clean, lightweight interior
use `strong` only for major section breaks where a clear, prominent boundary is needed
let whitespace do the work first — add a divider only when spacing alone is insufficient to convey separation
dont: overuse dividers — whitespace alone often suffices (per VQ whitespace guidance)
use dividers inside dense lists where they add visual clutter instead of clarity
use `strong` dividers between every item — it creates visual heaviness and breaks scanning flow
a11y: Dividers are non-interactive and do not receive keyboard focus
They should not appear in the tab order
interaction: Non-interactive display element \u2014 no pointer events by default
When placed inside an interactive container, inherits parent interaction
assembly: The `spacing` prop controls vertical padding above and below the divider line — use it to manage visual proximity between surrounding content groups
Low spacing (0–2): tight proximity — content is closely related (e.g., within a card section)
whenToUse: To separate logical content groups within a card or section
To create visual breaks between list items or stacked modules
To reinforce content hierarchy in dense layouts
whenNotToUse: When whitespace alone provides sufficient separation — avoid redundant dividers
Inside dense, tightly-packed lists where dividers add visual noise
As a decorative element — dividers should serve a structural purpose
Between full-width page sections — use spacing tokens and background changes instead

do: use Toggle for settings that take immediate effect — no "Save" button needed
label toggles with positive phrasing ("Show previews", not "Hide previews")
retain the checked/unchecked visual in disabled state so users understand current value
dont: use Toggle for multi-option selection — use Checkbox for "select all that apply" patterns
use Toggle within a form that has a "Submit" / "Save" action — use Checkbox instead
use negative label phrasing — "Disable X" toggles are confusing when the switch is "on"
a11y: Toggle must be focusable via `Tab` key
`Space` key toggles the checked state
interaction: Click/tap: Toggles between on and off states; thumb slides and track color changes
Keyboard: `Space` toggles the state when focused
assembly: Place toggles consistently (right-aligned in settings rows, or left-aligned with label following)
Use `small` spacing between the toggle switch and its label
whenToUse: To enable/disable a setting or feature with immediate effect
For binary preferences (on/off, show/hide, enable/disable)
When the action does not require confirmation before taking effect
whenNotToUse: For selecting one option from a group — use Radio button
For multi-option selection — use Checkbox
For triggering a one-time action — use Button
When the change requires a separate "Save" step — use Checkbox within a form

do: use `Default` style for standard filter bars — it provides clear interactivity without excess visual weight
use `Selected` style to clearly indicate which filters are currently applied
use `Dropdown?: True` when a filter category has sub-options (e.g., "Date" → Last week / Last month / Custom)
dont: use a single dropdown filter if that is the only filter for the given content — unless space constraints warrant this
use `Selected` style as a default/rest state — it should only indicate an active filter
mix filter item styles arbitrarily within the same filter bar — use consistent styling per group
a11y: Filter items must be focusable via `Tab` key
`Enter` or `Space` activates the filter (or opens the dropdown if `Dropdown?: True`)
interaction: Click/tap (non-dropdown): Toggles the filter on/off; switches between Default and Selected style
Click/tap (dropdown): Opens a sub-menu flyout with additional filter options
assembly: Arrange filter items horizontally in a filter bar, with horizontal scroll for overflow
Use `xx-small` spacing between filter items
whenToUse: To filter or refine search results by category, attribute, or facet
In horizontal filter bars above content grids
For faceted navigation in sidebar or inline filter groups
When a filter can expand into a sub-menu (use `Dropdown?: True`)
whenNotToUse: For triggering one-time actions — use Button
For navigation between content panels — use Tab
For binary on/off settings — use Toggle
For form field selection — use Dropdown or Radio button

do: show thumbnails only when images provide meaningful additional context (entities, products)
highlight the matching portion of the suggestion text to show relevance
limit the visible suggestion count (5-8 items) to avoid overwhelming the user
dont: show thumbnails for generic text queries — reserve for entity/rich suggestions
display stale or irrelevant suggestions — freshness and relevance are critical
block the search input when the suggestion list is open
a11y: Suggestion list must use `role="listbox"` with individual items as `role="option"`
`↑` `↓` arrow keys navigate between suggestions; `Enter` selects
interaction: Hover: Background shifts to hover state on pointer enter
Click/tap: Executes the suggested search query
assembly: Place Query suggestion items horizontally between the Section title and ML body content
Arrange items in a horizontal row with consistent gap between chips
whenToUse: As autocomplete items in a search box dropdown
For search query predictions and recent searches
When rich suggestions with thumbnails add meaningful context
whenNotToUse: For suggested actions or follow-ups — use Suggestion chip
For related searches at the bottom of results — use Related search pill
For filtering content — use Filter item

do: close the menu when an item is selected or the user clicks outside
indicate the currently selected option (if applicable) with a check mark or highlight
use keyboard navigation (arrow keys) to move between items
dont: open a menu from within another menu (no nested menus) unless absolutely necessary
use the menu for content display — it is for actions and navigation
put complex interactive elements (forms, sliders) inside menu items
a11y: Trigger element opens the menu on `Enter` or `Space`
Arrow keys (`Up`/`Down`) navigate between menu items
interaction: Open: Click/tap the trigger button to expand the menu flyout
Select: Click/tap a menu item to execute its action and close the menu
assembly: Menu floats below or adjacent to its trigger element
Menu width should accommodate the longest item label without truncation
whenToUse: For dropdown selection menus triggered by a button or control
For action menus (share, save, report, more options "⋯")
For navigation submenus
whenNotToUse: For contextual tooltips or previews — use Flyout
For modal content — use Overlay
For form field dropdowns with autocomplete — use a combobox pattern
For filters — use Filter item components

color.values: prose, primary, secondary, success, warning, danger

do: maintain clear typographic hierarchy — use one `xl` for hero content, a few `lg`/`md` for titles, and `sm`/`xs` for body and metadata
use `medium` weight for titles and headings, `normal` weight for body text — this maps to the `-strong` / regular token pattern
use `prose` as the default color for general content — it provides optimal readability on standard backgrounds
dont: use `danger` color for non-error text — it implies a critical issue and misleads users
mix sizes inconsistently within the same context (e.g., `xl` title next to `xs` title at the same hierarchy level)
use `medium` weight for all text — overusing bold dilutes hierarchy and adds visual noise, especially in dense layouts
a11y: All 6 color variants (`prose`, `primary`, `secondary`, `success`, `warning`, `danger`) meet contrast requirements in both light and dark themes
`secondary` (muted) color must maintain a minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio against card and page backgrounds
interaction: Non-interactive display element \u2014 no pointer events by default
When placed inside an interactive container, inherits parent interaction
assembly: Use `start` alignment for most content (LTR/RTL aware by default)
Match label sizes within the same context — don't mix `xl` and `xs` at the same hierarchy level
whenToUse: To display structured text that follows a defined typographic hierarchy (titles, descriptions, metadata)
As a form field label with optional required indicator
For semantic status text (success confirmations, warning notices, error messages)
To render body copy, attribution lines, or fine print with consistent styling
whenNotToUse: For interactive text that triggers navigation — use a link or Button instead
For status indicators that need background emphasis — use Badge
For long-form paragraph content with no structural role — use raw text elements
For section-level headings that carry semantic HTML weight — use heading elements directly

do: use `Neutral` Background Dim for standard content overlay loading
show a label when the user may not understand what is loading
use all 3 keyframes in implementation to create smooth animation
dont: show a spinner for operations under 300ms — it creates unnecessary visual noise
use `Accent` dim on neutral backgrounds — match the dim to the content area's style
leave a spinner running indefinitely — pair with timeout and error handling
a11y: Spinner region must use `role="status"` and `aria-live="polite"` to announce loading state
When `show label?: True`, the label text serves as the accessible description
interaction: Non-interactive: Spinner indicates loading in progress; no user action available
Animation: Circular rotation plays continuously until the loading operation completes
assembly: Center the spinner within its loading container
When using Background Dim, the overlay should cover the entire content area being loaded
whenToUse: To indicate content is loading or processing
As an overlay on existing content during refresh or update
When load time is indeterminate (no progress percentage available)
whenNotToUse: For determinate progress — use a progress bar
For skeleton/placeholder layouts — use skeleton screens
For brief operations (<300ms) — avoid showing any loading indicator

do: use the configuration (MD thickness, default gap) as the standard starting point for most contexts
match the progress bar width to its parent container for a clean, aligned appearance
include helper text to clarify what the progress represents — a bare bar without context is ambiguous
dont: use Progress Bar for indeterminate/unknown-duration loading — use or instead
use thickness for every bar — overuse of thick bars creates visual heaviness and reduces hierarchy
mix different thickness or gap variants within the same data group — consistency aids scanability
a11y: The progress bar should be implemented with `role="progressbar"` with `aria-valuenow`, `aria-valuemin`, and `aria-valuemax` attributes
Helper text should be programmatically associated with the bar via `aria-describedby` or equivalent
interaction: Non-interactive: Progress Bar is display-only; value is set by the system, not user input
Dynamic updates: Bar fill width animates proportionally when the current value changes
assembly: Progress bars should stretch to fill their container width (`layoutSizingHorizontal: FILL`)
Place inside cards or panels where the bar contextualizes content already present
whenToUse: To show progress toward a goal (e.g., "75% complete")
To visualize a metric within a known range (e.g., rating score, capacity)
To communicate data density or fill level inside cards
When the user needs a quick visual read of a numerical proportion
whenNotToUse: For indeterminate loading states — use **Spinner** instead
For content loading placeholders — use **Skeleton** instead
For step-based progress (e.g., multi-step wizard) — use a stepper or segmented indicator
For interactive value selection — use a slider or range input
When the value has no meaningful min/max boundary

style.values: loading, neutral, accent, error, success, warning

do: use `Priority` fill for errors and warnings — high-contrast background demands attention
use `Tint` fill for success and neutral messages — subtle appearance avoids disruption
show undo (`allow Undo?: True`) for any destructive action (delete, archive, remove)
dont: use toasts for critical errors that block the user — use a dialog or inline alert instead
stack more than 3 toasts simultaneously — it overwhelms and reduces readability
use `success` style for neutral/informational messages — use `Neutral` instead
a11y: Toast must be reachable via keyboard when it contains interactive elements (undo button)
Undo button must be focusable and activatable with `Enter` or `Space`
interaction: Auto-dismiss: Toast appears and automatically dismisses after a timed duration
Action link: Optional undo/action text is clickable before the toast dismisses
assembly: Position toasts at the bottom-center or top-center of the viewport, consistently
Stack multiple toasts vertically with `small` spacing between them
whenToUse: To confirm a completed action ("Saved", "Copied to clipboard")
To communicate errors or warnings that don't block interaction
To show transient loading status at the page level
When the user may want to undo an action immediately
whenNotToUse: For persistent or critical errors that require user action — use an inline error or dialog
For in-context loading — use Spinner within the loading container
For form validation — use inline field-level error messages
For promotional or marketing messages — use a banner component

do: use the beak to visually connect the Flyout to its trigger element
flip the pointing direction when the Flyout would overflow the viewport
dismiss the Flyout on outside click/tap and `Escape` key
on mobile, choose between lightweight (no backdrop) or overlay-like (backdrop + scroll lock) based on content importance
implement `aria-modal=true`, scroll lock, and focus trap when using overlay-like mobile approach
dont: nest Flyouts inside other Flyouts
place complex forms or multi-step flows inside a Flyout
use the overlay-like mobile approach without also implementing scroll lock and `aria-modal=true`
a11y: Flyout should open via keyboard interaction with the trigger (Enter, Space, or arrow keys)
`Escape` key dismisses the Flyout
interaction: Trigger action (click, hover, or keyboard) opens the Flyout near the trigger element
Click outside or press Escape dismisses the Flyout
Mobile overlay-like: tapping the backdrop dismisses the Flyout and page is non-scrollable
Mobile lightweight: tapping outside the Flyout dismisses it and page remains scrollable
assembly: Flyout should not overflow the viewport — reposition or flip direction if necessary
Beak should visually align with the center of the trigger element
whenToUse: For contextual information that appears on hover or click near a trigger element
For lightweight non-modal popups (tooltips, mini-previews, context menus)
When content should float over the page without dimming the background
On mobile with overlay-like approach: for important disclosures (e.g. AI disclaimers) that need user attention
whenNotToUse: For full modal dialogs with complex content — use Overlay
For side-panel detail views — use Drawer
For dropdown selection lists — use Menu
For full-screen or focus-trapping experiences

do: use consistent column widths across all rows
use compact for dense data displays within constrained card bodies
include a header row with distinct styling
dont: use tables for layout — use auto-layout frames instead
nest tables within tables
mix compact and standard rows within the same table
a11y: Tables must use proper table/thead/tbody/tr/th/td semantics in code
Header cells must use th with scope="col" or scope="row"
interaction: Table itself is a container — interaction states are on individual Table rows
Clickable rows highlight on hover and show pressed state on click
assembly: Table is composed of Table row sub-components — add/remove rows as needed
Use compact mode in data-dense contexts (e.g., specification tables inside cards)
whenToUse: To display structured data in rows and columns within cards or answer panels
For comparison tables, specification lists, or key-value data
When data has a consistent columnar structure
whenNotToUse: For simple key-value pairs — use Label or list patterns instead
For navigation or interactive lists — use List View
For layout purposes — use frames with auto-layout

do: use Checkbox for independent binary choices where users can select zero or more options
group related checkboxes vertically with a shared label to clarify the group's purpose
use the `Mixed` state on a parent checkbox when only some child options are selected
dont: use a checkbox for mutually exclusive choices — use Radio button instead
rely solely on color to indicate the checked state — always include the check icon
place checkboxes inline horizontally when there are more than three options
a11y: Checkbox must have `role="checkbox"` with `aria-checked` reflecting `true`, `false`, or `mixed`
Toggle with `Space`; focus ring uses the standard `focus outline` dependency
interaction: Click/tap: Toggles between unchecked, checked (and mixed when applicable)
Hover: Background shifts to `--smtc-background-ctrl-subtle-hover`; reverts on pointer leave
assembly: Align checkboxes to the start of text labels; maintain consistent spacing between items
Use `Mixed` state only in a parent–children hierarchy to reflect partial selection
whenToUse: To select one or more options from a list
For "select all" with partial selection (mixed state)
For agreement confirmations (e.g., terms and conditions)
whenNotToUse: For binary on/off settings — use Toggle instead
For mutually exclusive choices — use Radio button
For triggering immediate actions — use Button
For filtering content — use Filter item

do: use Radio button when exactly one option must be selected from a group of mutually exclusive choices
always pre-select a sensible default so the group is never empty
stack radio buttons vertically for readability when there are more than two options
dont: use radio buttons for multi-select scenarios — use Checkbox instead
present a single radio button in isolation; there should always be at least two options
change the circular indicator shape — it is the universal signifier for single-select
a11y: Wrap the group in a `<fieldset>` with a `<legend>` describing the question or context
Arrow keys move focus and selection within the group; `Tab` moves focus out of the group
interaction: Click/tap: Selects the targeted option and deselects the previously selected one
Hover: Background shifts to `--smtc-background-ctrl-subtle-hover`; reverts on pointer leave
assembly: Ensure all options in a group share the same `name` attribute for correct form behavior
Use consistent label alignment and spacing across all radio items in the group
whenToUse: To select one option from a set of 2+ mutually exclusive choices
In forms or settings where only one value is valid
When all options should be visible simultaneously
whenNotToUse: For binary on/off — use Toggle
For multi-select — use Checkbox
For selecting from a long list (5+) — consider Dropdown
For triggering actions — use Button

state.values: rest, hover, pressed, disabled, error, focus, read-only

do: always pair a Field with a visible label so users know what information to provide
use `helperText` to give additional context or formatting hints below the input
show `statusText` in the `error` state to explain what went wrong and how to fix it
dont: use placeholder text as a substitute for a label — it disappears on focus and hurts accessibility
stack error messages far from the field — keep `statusText` visually adjacent to the input
use `Borderless` style inside a form with other bordered fields — be consistent within a form
a11y: Associate the label with the input via `for`/`id` or by nesting; use `aria-describedby` for helper/status text
Error state must set `aria-invalid="true"` and link the error message with `aria-errormessage`
interaction: Click/tap on the field or label: Places cursor in the input and transitions to `focus` state
Hover: Border shifts to `--smtc-stroke-ctrl-hover`; reverts on pointer leave
assembly: Place label above the field; helper text below; status text below helper text when both are present
In forms, align all Field components to a consistent width for visual order
whenToUse: For any text input field within a form or control panel
When a field needs label, helper text, or validation status feedback
Inside composed form components (Date picker, search, filters)
whenNotToUse: For buttons or toggle controls — use Button or Toggle
For dropdown selection — use Menu or combobox patterns
For search queries in the Page header — the header has its own search bar component

do: group related fields together inside a single Form to provide clear submission context
use a primary Button at the end of the form for the submit action
validate all fields before submission and surface errors inline next to their respective Fields
dont: nest forms inside other forms — this is invalid HTML and leads to unpredictable behavior
hide the submit button until all fields are filled — keep it visible but disabled if needed
use a Form wrapper for a single standalone field that doesn't require submission
a11y: Use a `<form>` element with a descriptive `aria-label` or a visible heading to identify the form's purpose
Ensure pressing `Enter` in a text field submits the form when a submit button is present
interaction: Submit: Triggered by clicking the submit button or pressing `Enter` in a focused field
Validation: Inline errors appear on blur or on submit for each invalid Field
assembly: Stack Fields vertically with consistent gap using `--smtc-gap-between-content-medium`
Place action buttons (submit / cancel) at the bottom of the form, aligned to the end
whenToUse: To group related form controls (login forms, search filters, settings panels)
When form elements need consistent vertical spacing and alignment
As the semantic `<form>` wrapper in SDL output
whenNotToUse: For general content layout — use Basic or Section
For a single standalone input with no surrounding form context
For non-interactive content grouping

do: use `Single` selection for one-off dates (e.g., birthdate) and `Range` for start/end intervals
pre-populate the field with a sensible default date when context allows (e.g., today's date)
display the calendar flyout below or above the field depending on available viewport space
dont: force users to pick dates only from the calendar — allow manual text entry as well
use Date picker for time-only selection; combine with a separate time input when both are needed
open the calendar on page load — let the user explicitly trigger it
a11y: The trigger field must be keyboard accessible; `Enter`/`Space` opens the calendar flyout
Arrow keys navigate days within the calendar grid; `Escape` closes the flyout and returns focus to the trigger
interaction: Click/tap on the field or calendar icon: Expands the calendar flyout
Date selection: Clicking a day in `Single` mode selects it and closes the flyout; in `Range` mode the first click sets the start and the second click sets the end
assembly: Pair with a Field component for label, helper text, and error handling
Calendar flyout should be positioned using `--smtc-shadows-flyout` for elevation
whenToUse: For date selection in forms, filters, or settings panels
When users need to select a specific date or date range
Inside Drawer or Overlay panels for date-based filtering
whenNotToUse: For time-only selection — use a time picker pattern
For simple text input of dates — use Field with date formatting
For date display (non-interactive) — use plain text

do: use Dropdown when the list of options is too long for inline radio buttons or checkboxes (roughly 5+ items)
display a meaningful placeholder or default selection so users understand the expected input
keep option labels concise — one line each — to avoid overflow in the flyout
dont: use a Dropdown for fewer than three options — inline radios or a toggle are more efficient
auto-submit on selection change — let users review their choice before taking action
nest Dropdowns inside other Dropdowns — flatten the hierarchy instead
a11y: The trigger must have `role="combobox"` with `aria-expanded` and `aria-haspopup="listbox"`
Arrow keys navigate options; `Enter` selects; `Escape` closes the listbox and restores focus to the trigger
interaction: Click/tap on the trigger: Expands the option listbox flyout below or above the trigger
Option selection: Clicking an option selects it, updates the trigger label, and collapses the flyout
assembly: Pair with a Field component for consistent label, helper text, and error state support
Position the flyout using `--smtc-shadows-flyout` for proper elevation
whenToUse: To select one option from a moderate-to-large set (5+ items)
When screen space doesn't permit showing all options simultaneously
For form fields requiring a single selection from a predefined list
whenNotToUse: For 2-4 options that can all be visible — use Radio button
For multi-select — use Checkbox group
For navigation between views — use Tab
For filtering content — use Filter item with `Dropdown?: True`

do: use Avatar to represent a person or entity with a photo, initials, or generic icon
provide `fallbackInitial` so a meaningful letter is shown when the image fails to load
use `supportingText` for the person's name or role when displayed alongside the avatar
dont: use Avatar for decorative thumbnails or product images — use Image or Thumbnail instead
display more than one initial character — keep it to a single letter for legibility at small sizes
override the circular shape for person avatars — circles are the convention for people
a11y: If the avatar is informative, provide `alt` text describing the person; if decorative, use `alt=""`
When used as a link or button, ensure the interactive wrapper has a descriptive accessible name
interaction: Static by default — no hover or click behavior unless wrapped in an interactive element
When linked: focus ring appears on the wrapper; click navigates to the profile or detail page
assembly: Pair avatar with `supportingText` using `--smtc-gap-between-content-xx-small` for consistent spacing
Maintain uniform sizes within a list of avatars for visual alignment
whenToUse: To display a user or entity profile image in article bylines or content cards
For author bylines in articles and content cards
For entity identification in comment threads and social contexts
whenNotToUse: For general image previews — use Thumbnail
For brand logos or icons — use a dedicated icon or logo component
For decorative imagery — use an image container

do: use `inline` variant for citations embedded within flowing text (e.g., superscript numbers)
use `in-card` variant when citations are displayed as a standalone reference list on a card
show the source title and domain so users can assess credibility before clicking
dont: hide citation sources behind a hover-only interaction — they should be discoverable on tap/click too
display an excessive number of inline citations in one sentence — group or summarize instead
use Citation for general links or navigation — it is specifically for source attribution
a11y: Each citation link must have descriptive text (source title) accessible to screen readers
The flyout that reveals source details should be dismissible with `Escape` and manage focus correctly
interaction: Click/tap on an inline citation number: Opens a flyout or tooltip with the source title, URL, and snippet
Hover on `in-card` citation: Subtle background highlight on the citation row
assembly: Position inline citations as superscript numbers immediately after the referenced claim
In the `in-card` variant, stack sources vertically with consistent spacing
whenToUse: **Inline** — Attribute individual claims within AI/Copilot-generated text. Place immediately after the referenced statement.
**In Card** — Show aggregated sources for an entire card's content. Place in the card footer or attribution area.
whenNotToUse: Do not use for general navigation links — use standard text links instead.
Do not use Citation for ad attribution — use Sponsored Slug + Ads menu icon.

do: use `inline` for short code references within a sentence (e.g., variable names or commands)
use `standalone` for multi-line code samples with syntax highlighting and a copy button
specify the `language` prop so syntax highlighting is applied correctly
dont: use Code Block for non-code content like logs or plain text — use a monospace text style instead
hide the copy button on standalone blocks — users expect to copy code samples easily
force horizontal scrolling for long lines when the container can wrap or expand
a11y: Wrap standalone code in a `<pre><code>` structure with `aria-label` describing the snippet purpose
The copy button must be keyboard accessible and announce 'Copied' status to screen readers
interaction: Copy button click: Copies the code content to the clipboard and shows a brief 'Copied' confirmation
Hover on standalone block: Reveals the copy button if it auto-hides on idle
assembly: Use `--mai-smtc-corner-card-default` for the outer container rounding of standalone blocks
Apply `--bing-smtc-text-global-body3-mono` for the monospace font inside the code area
whenToUse: To display formatted code snippets in AI/Copilot answers or documentation content
**Inline** — inside an accented card container for short, quick code examples within running text
**Standalone** — as an individual answer block between body text for multi-line code
whenNotToUse: For general preformatted text that is not code — use Markdown instead
For single inline code tokens within a sentence — use a styled `<code>` text run, not the full component
For editable code input — this is display-only

do: use Markdown to render structured AI-generated or user-authored content with headings, lists, and links
apply the standard typography scale (title1/2, subtitle1/2, body1/2/3) for consistent hierarchy
separate content sections with `--smtc-stroke-divider-default` for visual clarity
dont: override the built-in typography tokens with custom font sizes — stick to the design system scale
inject raw HTML inside Markdown content without sanitizing it first
use Markdown for single-line labels or short strings — use a Label or text atom instead
a11y: Rendered headings must follow a logical order (h1 → h2 → h3) for screen-reader navigation
Links within Markdown must have descriptive text — avoid bare URLs or 'click here'
interaction: Static content — no direct interaction on the Markdown container itself
Embedded links and buttons within the slot follow their own interaction patterns
assembly: Use `--smtc-gap-between-content-x-small` between paragraphs and list items for readability
Apply `--smtc-stroke-divider-default` between major content sections (e.g., before h2 headings)

tokens.typography: title1, title2, subtitle1, subtitle2, body1, body2, body3

whenToUse: To render AI/Copilot-generated markdown content (headings, lists, code blocks, links, emphasis)
To display content that streams in progressively during generation
For rich text answers in conversational or chat-based SERP experiences
whenNotToUse: For plain, unformatted text — use Label instead
For user-authored content that doesn't use markdown formatting
For static HTML content that doesn't originate from a generation pipeline
For short single-line text — use Label or body typography directly

do: use Thumbnail for small preview images of content such as articles, videos, or products
use `rounded` shape for general content thumbnails; use `square` for grid-aligned layouts
ensure the image is appropriately cropped to fill the container without distortion
dont: use Thumbnail for person avatars — use Avatar instead for circular people imagery
scale thumbnails beyond their intended size — they are optimized for small previews
display thumbnails without a fallback state — provide a placeholder for broken images
a11y: Provide descriptive `alt` text if the thumbnail conveys information; use `alt=""` if purely decorative
When the thumbnail is a link, ensure the wrapping anchor has an accessible name
interaction: Static by default — no hover or click behavior unless wrapped in a link or button
When linked: focus ring appears on the wrapper; click navigates to the detail page
assembly: Align thumbnails consistently within card layouts — use the same `size` across a list
Place thumbnails at the leading edge of a horizontal card or at the top of a vertical card
whenToUse: To display image previews in search result cards
For user avatars or entity images within grid layouts
To show video previews with a play indicator
For favicon-size icons alongside source attribution
whenNotToUse: For decorative backgrounds — use a background image container
For user identity — use Avatar (which adds placeholder and label support)
For full-width hero images — use an image card or media block

do: always display the Sponsored Slug on paid-placement cards to clearly distinguish ads from organic results
use `image` placement when the slug overlays a card image; use `title-row` when it sits next to the title
enable `showAdsMenu` to give users access to ad feedback options (e.g., 'Why this ad?')
dont: hide or obscure the sponsored label — transparency is required for compliance and trust
style the slug to look like organic content — it must be visually distinct
place multiple Sponsored Slugs on the same card
a11y: The slug text ('Ad' or 'Sponsored') must be readable by screen readers
The ads menu trigger must be keyboard accessible and announce its expanded/collapsed state
interaction: Ads menu click: Opens a contextual menu with options like 'Why this ad?' and 'Report ad'
Static label: The 'Sponsored' / 'Ad' text has no direct interaction
assembly: In `image` placement, position the slug at the bottom-left of the image with a semi-transparent background
In `title-row` placement, position the slug inline before or after the card title
whenToUse: To label any paid or sponsored content on the SERP
As a required advertising disclosure element
When users need to distinguish organic from paid results
whenNotToUse: For AI content disclosure — use Ai disclaimer
For ad menu access — use Ads menu icon (pair both together)
For general status badges or tags — use a badge component

do: always show an AI disclaimer alongside AI-generated content so users know it may contain errors
use `top-right` placement for card-level disclaimers; use `inline` when the disclaimer follows a text block
pair the disclaimer with the InfoSparkle icon to reinforce the AI-generated nature of the content
dont: hide the AI disclaimer behind a hover or click — it must be persistently visible
use the disclaimer on human-authored content — it is strictly for AI-generated material
override the secondary foreground color — the muted style is intentional to avoid alarm
a11y: The disclaimer text must be readable by screen readers as part of the content flow
Do not rely on the icon alone to convey meaning — the text must be sufficient on its own
interaction: Static — no direct interaction; the disclaimer is informational only
InfoSparkle icon is decorative and does not respond to click or hover
assembly: Place the disclaimer at the top-right of a card or inline below AI-generated text
Use `--smtc-gap-between-content-x-small` between the icon and the disclaimer text
whenToUse: Below or alongside any AI-generated content (summaries, answers, suggestions)
In Copilot response areas to set user expectations about content reliability
Where regulatory or policy requirements mandate AI content disclosure
whenNotToUse: For general informational notices — use Toast (Neutral) or inline text
For error or warning messages — use Toast with the appropriate semantic style
For sponsored content labeling — use Sponsored Slug

do: use Related search pill to suggest alternative or follow-up search queries to the user
show the search icon to reinforce that tapping the pill triggers a new search
keep query text concise — truncate with ellipsis if it exceeds the available width
dont: use Related search pill for navigation links that are not search queries
display more than 8–10 pills in a single row — use Horizontal Scroller for overflow
style pills to look like primary action buttons — they are low-emphasis suggestions
a11y: Each pill must be a focusable element (link or button) with the query text as its accessible name
Ensure the pill row is scrollable via keyboard when overflow occurs
interaction: Click/tap: Executes the search query and navigates to the results page
Hover: Background transitions to `--smtc-background-ctrl-subtle-hover`
assembly: Arrange pills in a horizontal row with consistent spacing; wrap or scroll on overflow
Place related search pills below the main results or at the end of an answer section
whenToUse: To display related search suggestions at the bottom of SERP results
In grid layouts for browse-and-discover patterns
For topic exploration adjacent to current search results
whenNotToUse: For inline suggestions within the search box — use Query suggestion
For quick-reply or action suggestions — use Suggestion chip
For filtering results — use Filter item

do: use Suggestion chip to offer contextual follow-up actions or prompts the user can take
keep labels short and action-oriented so users can scan quickly
group related suggestion chips together in a horizontal row for easy comparison
dont: use Suggestion chip for permanent navigation — it is for ephemeral, context-aware suggestions
mix suggestion chips with primary buttons in the same row — they serve different purposes
disable suggestion chips without explanation — remove them if they are not applicable
a11y: Each chip must be a focusable button with the label as its accessible name
Focus state uses the standard `focus outline` dependency for a visible focus ring
interaction: Click/tap: Triggers the suggested action (e.g., sends a follow-up prompt or applies a filter)
Hover: Background transitions to `--smtc-background-ctrl-subtle-hover`; border shifts to `--smtc-stroke-ctrl-hover`
assembly: Arrange chips in a horizontal row; allow wrapping on narrow viewports
Place suggestion chips below AI-generated answers or in conversational UI contexts
whenToUse: To present suggested queries or follow-up actions
In suggestion chip lists for search refinement
For conversational UI quick-reply options
whenNotToUse: For filtering content — use Filter item
For navigation between views — use Tab
For primary actions — use Button
For displaying related search terms in a grid — use Related search pill

whenToUse: As the primary container for answer-type SERP results (knowledge panels, quick answers, rich snippets)
When content needs an accented surface to stand out from the page background
For full-bleed content layouts (padding=none) such as embedded maps or media
When answer content length is unpredictable (height=flexible)
whenNotToUse: For image-dominated content — use Image Card
For video-specific playback experiences — use Video Card
For file/link attachments — use Attachment Card
As a nested card inside another card — use a secondary surface (`background-card-on-secondary-default-`)
Answer Card.overview: The Answer Card is the primary card container for SERP answer experiences. It wraps rich answer content — text blocks, lists, tables, media, and interactive elements — within a consistent card surface. Two background styles (accented and neutral) allow designers to create visual hierarchy on the pag

whenToUse: For file attachment results (PDFs, documents, spreadsheets, presentations)
For supplementary content links associated with a primary answer
When users need to select/toggle one or more items from a list of attachments
For downloadable resource cards
whenNotToUse: For primary image or video content — use Image Card or Video Card
For full answer experiences — use Answer Card
For navigation links — use Button (ghost or outline variant)
For filter/tag selection — use Filter Item or Toggle
Attachment Card.overview: The Attachment Card presents file attachments or supplementary linked content as a compact, interactive card. It is unique among card components in supporting a `selected` state (toggle selection) and a dedicated `focus` state for keyboard accessibility. Five states cover the full interaction lifecy

whenToUse: Embedding interactive sub-cards inside parent cards (e.g., restaurant listings, product items)
Any card-in-card pattern requiring hover/press/select states
Reusable card module across different answer experiences
Card body / nested Card.overview: Card body / nested Card is a reusable card-within-card component for embedding interactive content inside parent cards. It supports theme variants (accent/nonAccent), a selection state, and 4 interaction states (rest, hover, pressed, focused). Used as a shared module across different card scenarios.

whenToUse: Multi-page content within a card (e.g., image galleries, step-by-step guides)
Paginated results that don't fit in a single view
Card-level carousels distinct from section-level Horizontal Scroller
Card body / page by page carousel.overview: Card body / page by page carousel is a paginated carousel component for displaying content across multiple slides within a card. It supports standard (padded) and full-bleed (edge-to-edge) styles with 4 responsive width breakpoints. Each slide is a configurable slot for custom content.

whenToUse: To progressively reveal supplementary content (answers, details, metadata)
Inside horizontal scrollers or stacked accordion layouts
For "see more" patterns where content should stay in context
whenNotToUse: For primary navigation — use Tab or Navigation Link
For toggling a boolean state — use Toggle
For content that should always be visible — use a standard Card
For media-heavy expandable content — use Expansion Card / Image or Horizontal/Vertical attachment variants
Expansion Card / Default.overview: The default Expansion Card is a compact interactive surface that reveals additional content on click or tap. It serves as the base building block for accordion-like patterns and progressive-disclosure flows — users interact to expand or collapse a content area.

whenToUse: When expandable content benefits from a visual preview alongside text (e.g., product thumbnails, video stills, entity images)
For horizontal layouts inside scrollers where media context aids scanning
When a persistent `selected` state is needed to indicate the user's choice
whenNotToUse: For text-only expandable content — use Expansion Card / Default
When the media should appear above/below the text — use Expansion Card / Vertical Attachment
When the image IS the primary content — use Expansion Card / Image
Expansion Card / Horizontal Attachment.overview: The Horizontal Attachment variant of the Expansion Card pairs a side-mounted media element (thumbnail, image, or icon) with text content. The media sits to the left or right of the card body, making it ideal for content where a visual preview accompanies expandable detail. A `scale` property control

whenToUse: When the image IS the content — photo galleries, visual search results, image previews
For visual-first layouts where text is secondary to the imagery
When a persistent `selected` state is needed to mark user choice in a visual grid
whenNotToUse: When text content is equally or more important than the image — use Vertical or Horizontal Attachment variants
For text-only expandable content — use Expansion Card / Default
For layouts where a fixed media ratio with structured text is needed — use Expansion Card / Horizontal Attachment
Expansion Card / Image.overview: The Image variant of the Expansion Card is dominated by image content — the visual is the primary element, with minimal or overlay text. It is designed for image-rich expandable experiences where the photo, illustration, or graphic is the main hook. Interaction reveals additional detail, metadata, o

whenToUse: When a stacked image-over-text (or text-over-image) layout best represents the content
For visual-first cards where the media preview needs vertical prominence
When an explicit chevron indicator is needed to signal expandability
whenNotToUse: When the media should sit beside the text — use Expansion Card / Horizontal Attachment
When the image IS the primary content with minimal text — use Expansion Card / Image
For text-only expandable content — use Expansion Card / Default
Expansion Card / Vertical Attachment.overview: The Vertical Attachment variant of the Expansion Card places a media element (image, thumbnail, or graphic) above or below the text content in a stacked layout. An optional chevron button provides an explicit expand/collapse affordance. This variant is suited for content where the visual should be v

whenToUse: For side-by-side image comparison (before/after, option A vs. option B)
When two related images must be viewed together for context
For dual-image visual answers where both images have equal weight
For paired search results (e.g., two product images, two location photos)
whenNotToUse: For more than 2 images — use Multi Images Carousel Card or Image Card with `feature=slider`
For a single image — use Image Card
For images with extensive metadata — use Image Card (`default` or `single Source` feature)
For images of different importance levels — use Image Card for the primary, separate component for secondary
Hero-image Card.overview: The Hero-image card is a specialized card layout that presents exactly two images side by side within a single card container. It has no variant properties — a single, fixed-layout component optimized for dual-image presentation. Use it for comparison, before/after, or paired-image contexts where se

whenToUse: When the primary content is an image or photo
For image search results, gallery grids, or visual stories
When metadata needs to overlay the image (e.g., attribution on photos)
For carousel/slider experiences within a single card
whenNotToUse: For video content — use Video Card
For answer/text-dominant content — use Answer Card
For multi-image galleries with slide navigation — use Multi Images Carousel Card
For side-by-side image comparison — use Hero-image card
Image Card.overview: The Image Card is a visually-driven card component where imagery is the dominant content element. Six feature variants cover a range of image presentation patterns — from standard thumbnail cards to overlay-style metadata, and in-card sliders. It supports optional secondary action buttons, autoplay

feature.values: default, action-button, center-icon, overlay-meta-data, single-source, slider

whenToUse: For local search results that include geographic context
For direction/navigation results between locations
When a zoomed/enlarged map view is needed to highlight a specific area (`scale up`)
In answer modules where geographic context enhances the response
whenNotToUse: For static map images without interactivity — use Image Card with a map screenshot
For address-only content without visual map — use a text-based Answer Card
For satellite/aerial imagery — use Image Card with appropriate imagery treatment
Map Card.overview: The Map Card embeds geographic/map content within a card container. Two property axes control the card: the map presentation type (direction vs. scale up) and the theme mode (light/dark). The `Light mode?` property mirrors the Button `switchable` style pattern — maps respond to the page's light/dark

whenToUse: For image galleries or photo collections within a single card
When multiple images relate to the same topic and should be browsed sequentially
For visual answer experiences requiring slide-by-slide exploration
When horizontal space is limited and a carousel is preferable to a grid
whenNotToUse: For a single image — use Image Card
For exactly 2 images side-by-side — use Hero-image card
For video content — use Video Card
For in-card image sliders within a different card type — use Image Card with `feature=slider`
Multi Images Carousel Card.overview: The Multi Images Carousel Card presents a navigable series of images within a single card container. Three slide variants represent the navigation state: first slide, middle slides (2+), and last slide. Navigation controls and indicators adapt based on the current position — first slide hides the \

whenToUse: Quick fact answer panels displaying entity attributes (e.g., university info)
Structured key-value data in a grid format
Comparison tables within answer cards
Splits card.overview: Splits Card is a grid-based card layout that splits content into a matrix of cells — from a single row or column (1×2, 2×1) up to a 4×4 grid. Used for structured data presentation like quick facts, entity attributes, or comparison tables. Supports accent and nonAccent styles with configurable paddin

whenToUse: As a card type inside ML body / Short video answer sections
For displaying short-form vertical video content (TikTok-style) in SERP results
When video content needs hover-to-preview behavior with source attribution
whenNotToUse: For standard horizontal video embeds — use standard video card patterns
For image content — use image card patterns
For live/streaming content — different UI treatment needed
Tok Video.overview: Tok Video is a card type used inside the **ML body / Short video answer** section. It displays vertical portrait video thumbnails with optional time overlay and play button, and is typically arranged in a horizontally scrollable row.

whenToUse: For video search results and video answer modules
When a video thumbnail with play affordance is the primary content
For split-layout video + text content (`divided area`)
When video metadata needs to overlay the thumbnail (`overlay Meta Data`)
whenNotToUse: For static image content — use Image Card
For audio-only content — use a custom content card
For video playlists or multi-video carousels — consider Multi Images Carousel Card or a scroller pattern
For live-streaming indicators — extend with badge overlays from Image Card patterns
Video Card.overview: The Video Card presents video content with a thumbnail preview and associated metadata. Three feature variants handle different layout relationships between the video thumbnail and text content — from standard layouts to overlay metadata and split-area designs. It is the primary card type for video

whenToUse: As the inner layout primitive inside a larger component
When you need a simple two-slot flex container (horizontal or vertical)
To compose custom card or section internals
whenNotToUse: Directly in page-level layouts — use Section or Card containers instead
For form-specific grouping — use Form
For list-based repeated layouts — use List View
Basic.overview: Basic is a foundational layout container that arranges child content in either a horizontal row or vertical column. It exposes two body slots for composing content side-by-side or stacked. This is a **low-level building block** — it is not typically used directly by consumers but serves as the struc

whenToUse: As the repeating list container for vertically stacked content (search result rows, dropdown items, settings lists)
When consistent inter-item spacing is required across a list
Inside cards, containers, or dropdown flyouts to hold a group of ListViewItems
whenNotToUse: For a single standalone card or content block — use Card or Basic
For horizontal item arrangements — use Basic with `direction=row`
For form field grouping — use Form
List View.overview: List View is a vertical list container that controls the spacing (gap) between repeated child items via its `gap` variant. It exposes a body SLOT for ListViewItem instances or custom content. The gap creates visual proximity between items — dividers are NOT used inside ListView; they separate groups

whenToUse: As a row item inside List View for action menus (kebab/more menus, context menus)
Inside the Menu component for all action row items
When menu rows need an optional L2 (sub-menu) level
whenNotToUse: As a standalone component outside of a List View
For selection lists — use List view elements / Option instead
When rows need custom layout beyond label + icon + L2 chevron
List view elements / Menu item.overview: PAA (People Also Ask) Answer is the most complex ML Body module. It renders an expandable Q&A list inline within the algo mainline, combining a Section / title (heading), a List View body (expandable question rows), and a Section / footer (expand/collapse controls). Users can expand individual quest

whenToUse: For every standard search result in the algo mainline (organic and sponsored)
`type=general`: Organic algorithm-ranked results (blue links)
`type=sponsor`: Sponsored/ad results within the mainline
Toggle `end image` when the result has a relevant thumbnail
Toggle `attachment` when the result has expandable supplementary content (sitelinks, rich snippets)
whenNotToUse: For enriched result modules (video groups, PAA, mini magazines) — use the dedicated ML Body modules
As a standalone card outside the algo section — use Card components instead
For right rail content — this is a Zone A (Mainline) component only
ML Body / Basic Algo.overview: Basic Algo is the fundamental building block of the algo mainline. It represents a single algorithm result item — either an organic result (`type=general`) or a sponsored/ad result (`type=sponsor`). Optional properties add an end image (thumbnail on the right side) and an attachment (expandable cont

whenToUse: To present image results inline within the algo mainline when the query has image intent
When users may want to browse and refine image results without leaving the SERP
When image content is algorithmically relevant and benefits from a grid preview
whenNotToUse: As a standalone image gallery section — build a full Section (title + body type + footer) instead
For a single hero image — use Hero Image Card or Image Card directly
Outside of Zone A in the algo section — ML Body modules only slot into the mainline
When the query has no visual/image intent
ML Body / Images Answer.overview: Images Answer is an ML Body module that presents image results inline within the algo mainline. It combines a Section / title (with navigation chevron), a row of Query suggestion pills for refining the image search, and a two-row image grid displayed inside a Horizontal Scroller. A \

whenToUse: To insert a small curated card grid between standard algo results (Section / title → Body → Section / footer)
As an attachment inside a basic algo result (below the algo body)
When a compact magazine-style layout is needed inline within the mainline
whenNotToUse: As a full section body — use Section body / magazine instead
Outside of Zone A in the algo section — ML Body modules only slot into the mainline
For a single card or standalone card layout — use a Card component directly
ML Body / Mini Magazine.overview: Mini Magazine is an ML Body module that renders a miniature magazine-style card grid inline within the algo mainline. It appears between standard algorithm results in Zone A, providing a compact curated-content break within the search results stream. The component contains a single row instance that

whenToUse: To present news results inline within the algo mainline when the query has news/current-events intent
When a featured (hero) news article deserves prominent visual treatment
When multiple news sources are relevant and benefit from a grid preview
whenNotToUse: As a standalone news section — build a full Section (title + body type + footer) instead
For a single news link mixed into text results — use a standard algo result with news metadata
Outside of Zone A in the algo section — ML Body modules only slot into the mainline
When the query has no news/current-events intent
ML Body / News Answer.overview: News Answer is an ML Body module that presents news results inline within the algo mainline. It features a hero layout: a large featured news article (with image + source + headline + snippet) at the top, followed by a 2×2 grid of secondary news cards inside a Horizontal Scroller. A \

whenToUse: To present short-form vertical video results in a horizontally scrollable carousel within the algo mainline
When the search query has strong short video intent (trending, viral, social content)
When Tok video cards need a grouped section with a heading and swipeable navigation
whenNotToUse: For standard landscape video results — use ML body / video answer instead
As a standalone section outside the algo mainline — build a full Section (title + body type + footer)
For a single Tok video card mixed into text results — use a standard algo result
ML Body / Shorts Video Answer.overview: The Shorts Video Answer module is an ML Body unit that presents short-form vertical video results (TikTok-style) in a horizontally scrollable carousel within the algo mainline. It consists of a Section / title heading followed by a Horizontal Scroller containing Tok video cards.

whenToUse: To present a group of video results inline within the algo mainline
When video content is algorithmically relevant to the search query
When video results need a labeled group heading to distinguish them from standard text results
whenNotToUse: As a standalone video section — build a full Section (title + body type + footer) instead
For a single video card mixed into text results — use a standard algo result with video metadata
Outside of Zone A in the algo section — ML Body modules only slot into the mainline
ML Body / Video Answer.overview: Video Answer is an ML Body module that presents video results inline within the algo mainline. It combines a Section / title (for labeling the video group) with a List View-based body (for laying out video cards). This module appears between standard algorithm results in Zone A, giving video content

ML Body / Q&A Answer.overview: Q&A Answer is an ML Body module that renders an AI-generated answer with a Copilot-styled section title, expandable answer body (424px collapsed with gradient fade), and a horizontal source card carousel. The answer body features title/2-strong headings, subtitle/2-strong subheadings, and body/2 par

whenToUse: As the first child of any Section component
To label a content group (e.g., "Top stories", "People also ask", "Related")
When a section needs a subtitle or inline action (e.g., "See all")
whenNotToUse: As a standalone heading outside of a Section — use standard typography instead
For card-level titles — use card title patterns
For page-level headings — use the Page header
> **endProps slot:** Optionally shows tab-like pills (Wholepage tabs) that requery the page according to topics related to the answer.
Section / Title.overview: Section Title is the heading bar that sits at the top of every Section. It provides the section's label and optional subtitle or action link. Every section in the SERP begins with a Section Title, establishing content hierarchy and giving users a clear landmark for scanning.

whenToUse: As the last child of any Section component (after Section Title + body type)
When a section needs a CTA ("See more", redirect link)
When user feedback (thumbs up/down) should be collected for a section
When source attribution or footnotes are required
whenNotToUse: As a standalone footer outside a Section — use Page footer instead
For card-level actions — use card footer patterns
For standalone feedback — feedback only appears within Section Footer
